frameworks.name: neural manifold representation
frameworks.one_line: objects encoded as points on a low-dimensional neural manifold rearranged by new relational information

frameworks.name: connectionist neural network with stochastic gradient descent
frameworks.one_line: online SGD adapted to permit few-shot knowledge assembly in a NN model

frameworks.name: transitive inference
frameworks.one_line: novel objects learned within an ordered relation across two contexts

core_question: How does the brain rapidly reorganize neural codes for object relations when minimal new linking information reveals how previously separate sets connect?
key_assumptions: object relations can be read out from population-level neural geometry
knowledge assembly requires few-shot reorganization, not slow re-learning
BOLD signal geometry in frontoparietal cortex reflects relational structure
manipulations: two distinct learning contexts each containing a transitive order over novel objects
minimal exposure to linking information bridging the two contexts
key_findings: BOLD signals in dorsal frontoparietal cortex rearranged objects on the neural manifold after minimal linking exposure
the rearrangement was rapid and dramatic, consistent with few-shot assembly
an online-SGD-adapted neural network reproduced similar rapid knowledge assembly
interpretations: neural codes for relations can be reorganized in a few-shot manner once linking information arrives
standard learning theories under-specify this rapid assembly; an SGD variant is one mechanism that can support it
